1	# Second Brain Wiki Configuration
2	
3	This file configures the skill for Evan's environment. Edit paths and
4	projects as your work evolves.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Paths
9	
10	| Path | Value | Description |
11	|------|-------|-------------|
12	| Wiki directory | `~/second-brain/wiki/` | Where wiki pages live |
13	| Sources directory | `~/second-brain/sources/` | Raw source files stored here |
14	| Inbox directory | `~/Desktop/Second Brain Inbox/` | Drop files here for processing |
15	
16	---
17	
18	## Active Projects
19	
20	These are your current projects and areas of focus. The skill uses these
21	to find connections when ingesting new content.
22	
23	- **evaluator-super-agent** — automated prototype quality assessment using persona-driven walkthroughs, 7-dimension rubric scoring, actor/evaluator separation
24	- **prototype-creator** — generates clickable prototypes from RFEs, tiered AC classification, Playwright automation
25	- **agent-eval-harness** — framework for evaluating AI skill quality, eval.yaml configs, judges, scoring pipelines
26	- **ai-sdlc-pipeline** — three-phase pipeline: RFE Creator (WHAT) → Prototype Creator (SHOW) → Strat Creator (HOW)
27	- **my-cursor-claw** — personal workspace with 20+ skills, MCP connections, notetaking, automations
28	- **decision-kit** — Decision Driven Development toolkit, /strategize, /decide, /journal
29	- **automated-usability-testing** — simulated usability testing tool, persona YAML files, evaluate-flow protocol
30	- **archie** — UX research agent for the RHOAIUX team
31	- **rhoai-prototypes** — RHOAI prototype collection for evaluation
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Calendar Integration
36	
37	| Setting | Value |
38	|---------|-------|
39	| Calendar name | `primary` |
40	| Auto-ingest meetings | `true` |
41	| Minimum meeting duration | `15 min` |
42	| Require notes/transcript | `true` |
43	| Skip recurring without notes | `true` |
44	
45	Events that qualify for auto-ingestion:
46	- Have attached meeting notes or a description with substance (not just "Weekly sync")
47	- Have a linked Google Doc (agenda, transcript, action items)
48	- Duration is 15+ minutes
49	- Are not recurring meetings that had no notes added
50	
51	---
52	
53	## MCP Integrations
54	
55	| Integration | MCP Server | Used for |
56	|-------------|-----------|----------|
57	| Google Docs | `user-google-workspace` | Extracting doc content |
58	| Google Calendar | `user-google-workspace` | Meeting ingestion |
59	| Slack | `project-0-my-cursor-claw-slack` | Channel message ingestion |
60	
61	---
62	
63	## Cross-Link Priority
64	
65	When multiple connections exist, prioritize these categories (from the
66	wiki index):
67	
68	1. **Red Hat AI-SDLC** — pipeline components, RFE/prototype/strat flow
69	2. **Evaluation & Quality** — rubrics, heuristics, eval harness
70	3. **Tools & Skills** — decision kit, cursor claw, skill overlays
71	4. **Meetings** — meeting notes with action items and decisions
72	5. **Standups** — sprint progress and blockers
73	6. **Session Logs** — what was built and shipped
74

1	# Wiki Page Template
2	
3	Use this template when creating new wiki pages. Copy the structure,
4	fill in the bracketed fields.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Template
9	
10	```markdown
11	# <Page Title>
12	
13	<One-sentence summary of what this page covers.>
14	
15	## Key Points
16	
17	- <Bullet point 1: a concrete fact, concept, or takeaway>
18	- <Bullet point 2>
19	- <Bullet point 3>
20	- <Additional bullets as needed, max 10>
21	
22	## Related
23	
24	- [<Related Page Title>](<related-slug>.md) — <one-line reason for connection>
25	- [<Another Related Page>](<slug>.md) — <connection reason>
26	
27	## Sources
28	
29	- <path/to/source/file or URL where this came from>
30	- Ingested: <YYYY-MM-DD>
31	```
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Good Example
36	
37	```markdown
38	# Retrieval Augmented Generation
39	
40	Technique that grounds LLM responses in retrieved documents rather than
41	relying solely on training data.
42	
43	## Key Points
44	
45	- Combines a retriever (vector search) with a generator (LLM)
46	- Reduces hallucination by providing source documents as context
47	- Trade-off: retrieval quality limits generation quality
48	- Common stack: embeddings → vector DB → reranker → LLM prompt
49	- Works best when source corpus is well-chunked and up-to-date
50	
51	## Related
52	
53	- [Vector Databases](vector-databases.md) — the retrieval layer in RAG pipelines
54	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — uses embedding similarity for judge calibration
55	
56	## Sources
57	
58	- ~/second-brain/sources/what-is-rag.md
59	- Ingested: 2026-06-10
60	```
61	
62	---
63	
64	## Bad Example
65	
66	```markdown
67	# RAG
68	
69	Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique in artificial intelligence
70	that combines information retrieval with text generation. It was first proposed
71	by Lewis et al. in 2020. The basic idea is that instead of relying solely on
72	what a language model learned during training, you can augment it with relevant
73	documents retrieved at inference time.
74	
75	This approach has several advantages. First, it reduces hallucination because
76	the model can reference actual documents. Second, it allows the model to access
77	up-to-date information that wasn't in its training data. Third, it provides
78	transparency because you can trace responses back to source documents.
79	
80	There are several implementations of RAG available today...
81	```
82	
83	**Why it's bad:**
84	- Reads like a Wikipedia article, not a wiki node
85	- No bullet summaries (walls of prose)
86	- No Related section (isolated page, no connections)
87	- No Sources section (where did this come from?)
88	- Title is an acronym without expansion
89	- Contains filler ("There are several..." "This approach has...")
90

text: I notice there's already a `nielsens-10-usability-heuristics.md` page in your wiki. Let me read it and also fetch the URL content via curl.

content:
1	# Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics
2	
3	Foundational UX design principles by Jakob Nielsen (1994), refined from 249 usability problems. Used as the base rubric for heuristic evaluation and automated prototype assessment.
4	
5	## Key Points
6	
7	- **1 — Visibility of System Status**: Always inform users about what's happening; feedback should be timely and predictable — builds trust
8	- **2 — Match System to Real World**: Use language and concepts familiar to users, not internal jargon; follow real-world conventions and natural mapping
9	- **3 — User Control and Freedom**: Provide clear "emergency exits" (undo/redo, cancel); prevents users from feeling trapped by accidental actions
10	- **4 — Consistency and Standards**: Don't make users guess if different words/actions mean the same thing; follow platform conventions (Jakob's Law: users spend most time on other products)
11	- **5 — Error Prevention**: Prevent problems first; two error types — slips (unconscious, inattention) and mistakes (mental model mismatch); use constraints and good defaults
12	- **6 — Recognition Rather than Recall**: Make elements/actions/options visible; don't force users to remember info across interface sections; limited short-term memory
13	- **7 — Flexibility and Efficiency**: Provide accelerators (shortcuts, gestures) for experts while keeping the interface accessible to novices; allow customization
14	- **8 — Aesthetic and Minimalist Design**: Every extra info unit competes with relevant content; not about flat design — about keeping visual elements focused on user goals
15	- **9 — Help Recover from Errors**: Error messages in plain language, precisely identify the problem, constructively suggest a solution; use visual treatments to make errors noticeable
16	- **10 — Help and Documentation**: Best if no docs needed; when required — searchable, task-focused, concise, with concrete steps
17	- Originally developed with Rolf Molich (1990); refined via factor analysis of 249 problems (1994); unchanged since then
18	- Severity ratings 1–4 commonly used when applying heuristics to specific interfaces
19	
20	## Related
21	
22	- [Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) — applied these heuristics in an AI model comparison across Cursor, Claude Code, Gemini, and ChatGPT
23	- [UX Quality Rubric](ux-quality-rubric.md) — usability dimension is grounded in these heuristics
24	- [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) — automated prototype evaluation uses heuristics as scoring framework
25	- [Weekly Design Sync — July 1](weekly-design-sync-2026-07-01.md) — team adopted Nielsen's heuristics as base rubric for evaluations
26	
27	## Sources
28	
29	- https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ten-usability-heuristics/
30	- Ingested: 2026-07-09
31

input.command:
curl -sL "https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ten-usability-heuristics/" | python3 -c "
import sys, html, re
content = sys.stdin.read()
# Remove script/style tags
content = re.sub(r'<(script|style|nav|header|footer)[^>]*>.*?</\1>', '', content, flags=re.DOTALL|re.IGNORECASE)
# Extract main article content
article_match = re.search(r'<article[^>]*>(.*?)</article>', content, re.DOTALL|re.IGNORECASE)
if article_match:
    content = article_match.group(1)
else:
    # Try main tag
    main_match = re.search(r'<main[^>]*>(.*?)</main>', content, re.DOTALL|re.IGNORECASE)
    if main_match:
        content = main_match.group(1)
# Remove HTML tags but keep text
content = re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', ' ', content)
# Clean whitespace
content = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', content).strip()
# Decode HTML entities
content = html.unescape(content)
print(content[:8000])
" 2>/dev/null
input.description: Fetch and extract text from Nielsen's heuristics article

content: 7 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design Jakob Nielsen Jakob Nielsen April 24, 1994 · Updated Jan. 30, 2024 2024-01-30 Share Email article Share on LinkedIn Share on Twitter Summary:  Jakob Nielsen's 10 general principles for interaction design. They are called "heuristics" because they are broad rules of thumb and not specific usability guidelines. Thank you to Kelley Gordon for designing the visuals and posters included in this article. Thank you to Kate Moran and Feifei Liu for updating the heuristic names, descriptions, and examples. In This Article: 1: Visibility of System Status 2: Match Between the System and the Real World 3: User Control and Freedom 4: Consistency and Standards 5: Error Prevention 6: Recognition Rather than Recall 7: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use 8: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design 9: Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors 10: Help and Documentation Free 10 Heuristics Poster 1: Visibility of System Status The design should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate feedback within a reasonable amount of time. When users know the current system status, they learn the outcome of their prior interactions and determine next steps. Predictable interactions create trust in the product as well as the brand. Go one step further than definitions. Use the heuristics to defend and inform your design in our self-paced course, 10 Usability Heuristics — created by the team who brought you the framework first. What You'll Learn Example of Usability Heuristic #1: You Are Here indicators on mall maps show people where they currently are, to help them understand where to go next. Tips Communicate clearly to users what the system’s state is — no action with consequences to users should be taken without informing them. Present feedback to the user as quickly as possible (ideally, immediately). Build trust through open and continuous communication . Learn more Full article: Visibility of System Status 3-minute video about the Visibility Heuristic 2: Match Between the System and the Real World The design should speak the users' language. Use words, phrases, and concepts familiar to the user, rather than internal jargon. Follow real-world conventions, making information appear in a natural and logical order. The way you should design depends very much on your specific users. Terms, concepts, icons, and images that seem perfectly clear to you and your colleagues may be unfamiliar or confusing to your users. When a design’s controls follow real-world conventions and correspond to desired outcomes (called natural mapping ), it’s easier for users to learn and remember how the interface works. This helps to build an experience that feels intuitive. Example of Usability Heuristic #2: When stovetop controls match the layout of heating elements, users can quickly understand which control maps to which heating element. Tips Ensure that users can understand meaning without having to go look up a word’s definition. Never assume your understanding of words or concepts will match that of your users. User research will uncover your users' familiar terminology, as well as their mental models around important concepts. Learn more Full article: Match Between the System and the Real World 2-minute video: Match Between the System and the Real World 3: User Control and Freedom Users often perform actions by mistake. They need a clearly marked "emergency exit" to leave the unwanted action without having to go through an extended process. When it's easy for people to back out of a process or undo an action, it fosters a sense of freedom and confidence. Exits allow users to remain in control of the system and avoid getting stuck and feeling frustrated. Example of Usability Heuristic #3: Digital spaces need quick emergency exits, just like physical spaces do. Tips Support Undo and Redo . Show a clear way to exit the current interaction, like a Cancel button . Make sure the exit is clearly labeled and discoverable. Learn more Full article: User Control and Freedom 2-minute video: User Control and Freedom 4: Consistency and Standards Users should not have to wonder whether different words, situations, or actions mean the same thing. Follow platform and industry conventions. Jakob's Law states that people spend most of their time using digital products other than yours . Users’ experiences with those other products set their expectations. Failing to maintain consistency may increase the users' cognitive load by forcing them to learn something new. Example of Usability Heuristic #4: Checkin counters are usually located at the front of hotels. This consistency meets customers’ expectations. Tips Improve learnability by maintaining both types of consistency: internal and external. Maintain consistency within a single product or a family of products (internal consistency). Follow established industry conventions (external consistency). Learn more Full article: Consistency and Standards 3-minute video: Consistency and Standards 5: Error Prevention Good error messages are important, but the best designs carefully prevent problems from occurring in the first place. Either eliminate error-prone conditions, or check for them and present users with a confirmation option before they commit to the action. There are two types of errors: slips and mistakes . Slips are unconscious errors caused by inattention. Mistakes are conscious errors based on a mismatch between the user’s mental model and the design. Example of Usability Heuristic #5: ​​​​ Guard rails on curvy mountain roads prevent drivers from falling off cliffs. Tips Prioritize your effort: Prevent high-cost errors first, then little frustrations. Avoid slips by providing helpful constraints and good defaults . Prevent mistakes by removing memory burdens, supporting undo, and warning your users . Learn more Full article: Preventing User Errors 3-minute video: Error Prevention 6: Recognition Rather than Recall Minimize the user's memory load by making elements, actions, and options visible. The user should not have to remember information from one part of the interface to another. Information required to use the design (e.g. field labels or menu items) should be visible or easily retrievable when needed. Humans have limited short-term memories . Interfaces that promote recognition reduce the amount of cognitive effort required from users. Example of Usability Heuristic #6: It’s easier for most people to recognize the capitals of countries, instead of having to remember them. People are more likely to correctly answer the question Is Lisbon the capital of Portugal? rather than What’s the capital of Portugal? Tips Let people recognize information in the interface, rather than forcing them to remember (“recall”) it. Offer help in context , instead of giving users a long tutorial to memorize. Reduce the information that users have to remember. Learn more Full article: Recognition vs. Recall in UX 3-minute video: Recognition vs. Recall 7: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use Shortcuts — hidden from novice users — may speed up the interaction for the expert user so that the design can cater to both inexperienced and experienced users. Allow users to tailor frequent actions. Flexible processes can be carried out in different ways, so that people can pick whichever method works for them. Example of Usability Heuristic #7: Regular routes are listed on maps, but locals with knowledge of the area can take shortcuts. Tips Provide accelerators like keyboard shortcuts and touch gestures. Provide personalization by tailoring content and functionality for individual users. Allow for customization , so users can make selections about how they want the product to work. Learn more Full article: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use: The 7th Usability Heuristic Explained 3-minute video: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use 8: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design Interfaces should not contain information t

text:
This source is already in your wiki. The page **[Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics](~/second-brain/wiki/nielsens-10-usability-heuristics.md)** already exists and lists `https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ten-usability-heuristics/` as its source (ingested 2026-07-09).

The existing page covers all 10 heuristics with concise summaries and is cross-linked to 4 related pages:
- [Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md)
- [UX Quality Rubric](ux-quality-rubric.md)
- [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md)
- [Weekly Design Sync — July 1](weekly-design-sync-2026-07-01.md)

No new page created — this would be a duplicate. If you'd like me to **expand** the existing page with more detail (the article has per-heuristic tips and examples that aren't currently captured), I can do that instead.
